title: Repeat Last Change
subtitle: {key:.} — replay the last change. The single most useful key.
summary: The . command repeats the last *change* — exactly what you did, including any inserted text. It is what makes Vim feel like Vim.

blocks.text: If Vim has a single most-loved key, this is it. {key:.} replays the last change. Not the last keystroke, not the last motion — the last *change*. That distinction is the whole reason {key:.} feels magical.

blocks.title: What Counts as "a Change"
blocks.text:
Vim has a precise definition of *change* (and only changes can be dotted):

  • An entire Insert-mode session, from {key:i}/{key:a}/{key:o}/etc. through {key:Esc}, is **one change**. The text you typed is part of it.
  • Every operator+motion ({key:d}{key:w}, {key:c}{key:i}{key:p}, {key:>}{key:i}{key:p}) is **one change**.
  • Every single-shot edit ({key:x}, {key:r}{c}, {key:p}, {key:~}) is **one change**.
  • Pure motions and searches are **not changes**. {key:j}, {key:G}, {key:/}, {key:n} don't update the dot register.

Vim records the *commands and any typed text* of the last change in the **dot register** ({key:"}{key:.}). Pressing {key:.} replays them at the current cursor position.

So {key:c}{key:w}-type-`hello`-{key:Esc} is one change. {key:.} on a different word will change-word-to-`hello` again. {key:o}-type-a-line-{key:Esc} is one change. {key:.} opens another line and types the same content.

Search ({key:/}) is *not* a change, but it pairs with {key:.} beautifully: {key:/}{key:foo}{key:Enter}-{key:c}{key:w}-`bar`-{key:Esc} → {key:n}{key:.} {key:n}{key:.} {key:n}{key:.} walks you through every match. Find next; repeat the change. Two keys to refactor each occurrence.
{key:u} undoes one change. {key:.} repeats one change. Together they let you preview-and-undo refactors: do it once, see if you like it, {key:u} if not, {key:n}{key:.} if yes. Confidence at speed.

rows: Repeat last change with new count {n}
Paste the actual recorded change as text

blocks.title: Dot replays a change recipe, not keystrokes
blocks.text: {key:.} is not a keyboard macro. The editor records the last change — the operator, its motion or text object, its count, and (for inserts) the typed text — into a small struct. {key:.} re-executes that struct against the current cursor position. Pure motions and visual-only operations don't update the struct, which is why you can move freely between repeats without losing your dot.
